title: Black Tea on Cotton
stainChemistry: Black tea is higher in tannins than coffee—stronger hydrogen bonding to cotton cellulose. Tea stains brown more quickly as tannins oxidize (within hours). White cotton's chemical tolerance allows aggressive acid and oxidizing treatment. The mechanism is identical to coffee (tannin bonding, acid breaks bonds, oxidizers remove color), but tea oxidizes faster and requires prompt treatment.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flushes initial stain. Acetic acid breaks tannin-cellulose bonds immediately. H₂O₂ or bleach oxidizes remaining pigment. White cotton's robustness means all chemistry can be applied aggressively. Tea responds faster than coffee to these methods because tea tannins are slightly more soluble in acid.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold water from back, blot hard. Most fresh tea will flush out if treated immediately. Flip to front and continue. Water will turn tan/brown. Because tea oxidizes quickly, speed is important here—treat within the first few minutes if possible.

Apply vinegar directly to remaining tan/brown stain. Full strength safe on white cotton. Acidity breaks tannin bonds aggressively. Watch stain lighten noticeably. Tea tannins respond to acid very well. After 2-3 minutes, blot thoroughly.

If stain is brown or color remains, apply peroxide. Full strength safe. Watch oxidation happen—stain fades to colorless. For aged tea (very brown), 6% works faster than 3%. After 3-5 minutes, blot. Safe for white and light-colored cotton. For dark or colored fabrics, skip this step and proceed to NSD flush — test a hidden area first if unsure.

Flush thoroughly with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub. All oxidizer and enzyme residue must be fully removed before drying.
Rinse thoroughly to remove all acid, peroxide, and H₂O₂ for white/light fabrics only. Then wash in warm or hot water with regular detergent.
White cotton tolerates heat. Machine dryer or line dry—both fine.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Cold water flush, blot hard. Most comes out immediately.
REMAINING: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted), 2-3 minutes, blot. Then H₂O₂, 3-5 minutes, blot.
BROWN/AGED: H₂O₂ (3-6%) soak 30-60 min, blot thoroughly.
materialWarnings: DO NOT use bleach on colored cotton
escalation: Black tea on white cotton rarely needs professional care. Home treatment is fast and very effective.

household.name: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted)
household.note: Acidic spotter. Full strength on white cotton. Very effective on tea.

household.name: H₂O₂ (3-6%)
household.note: Oxidizes tannin color. Full strength safe on white cotton.

professional.name: StreeTAN
professional.note: Professional spotter. Rarely needed—home treatment works well.

customerExplanation: Black tea contains high levels of tannins that bond to cotton. Acid (vinegar) breaks the bonds. H₂O₂ oxidizes the brown color. White cotton's chemical robustness means we can use strong, fast chemistry.
commonMistakes: Delaying treatment—tea oxidizes quickly and becomes harder to remove
Not using acid first—acid is essential for breaking tannin-cotton bonds
Skipping peroxide—it removes the color completely
scienceNote: Tea tannins (from black tea) are complex polyphenolic compounds with even more hydroxyl groups than coffee tannins, making them highly polar and soluble in acidic solutions. This is why vinegar works so well on tea—better than on coffee. Tea oxidizes faster than coffee (within 1-2 hours), so aged tea stains brown quicker. This is partly why immediate treatment is more important for tea than coffee.
callPro: Black tea on white cotton rarely needs professional care.
solventNote: Professional: Cold water flush, then acetic acid (GAA or diluted vinegar) for immediate treatment. For brown/aged tea, H₂O₂ (3-6%) or sodium percarbonate. Bleach (0.5-1%, white only) works very fast. For aged brown stains, reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite 0.5%) for 10-15 min, then oxidize to colorless. Standard approach: acid → oxidize → oxidize.
